Thanks for the opening, but 7.12 is well above a level that works for our manufacturing economics. Based on our current cost structure and the volume consistency we can bring, I can open at 2.78 per unit as a realistic starting point from our side. If you have flexibility tied to volume bands or scheduling commitments, share that structure and we can see whether there is a path to narrow the gap. The supplier opened far above our workable range, so the reasonable move is to counter rather than accept or walk away. A low but credible first buyer anchor preserves room to negotiate while signaling that current pricing needs to move substantially to fit our margin requirements.

A counteroffer with a moderate concession preserves credibility, signals willingness to negotiate, and leaves room for further movement without ending discussions.

events.note: I see the market has tightened, but 6.64 is still far beyond what we can support in our production economics. To keep this moving, I can improve from 2.78 to 3.01 per unit if we can align on dependable volume and a workable delivery schedule. That reflects a real concession on our side while staying grounded in what we can responsibly buy at. The supplier's offer is far above the buyer's feasible range, so accepting is not reasonable. With the market disruption, a modest upward move is appropriate to show seriousness and preserve the negotiation, but the counter should remain well below the seller's number to protect margin and leave room for further bargaining.

I appreciate the movement to 3.46, especially with the repeat-volume angle, but that level is still too far below where we can responsibly commit supply. The broader market is supporting materially higher pricing, so I can make one more meaningful concession and come down to 4.22 per unit if we can pair it with steady call-offs and a reliable volume commitment. That narrows the gap substantially from our last position while still reflecting current market conditions and our production economics. The buyer moved up, so continuing the negotiation is preferable to ending it. With the latest market reference well above both parties' numbers and only one turn left, a firmer but still concessional counteroffer is the most reasonable move.

I hear your rationale, but 4.22 still doesn’t leave enough room for us to make the downstream economics work. We’ve already stretched materially from where we started, and with this being the last turn I can make a final move to 3.62 per unit if that secures dependable supply and clean execution on volume. That gives you another step up from our prior offer and is about as far as we can reasonably go while still supporting an ongoing program. There is still a large gap, but rejecting now would end the discussion without testing whether the supplier will take a last-turn compromise. The market reference is high, which weakens our leverage, yet the latest seller offer remains far from our economics, so a firm final counter is the most reasonable move.
